role_summary: Mother of Emmett Till (catalog external_figure page); the decision to hold an open-casket funeral at Roberts Temple Church of God in Christ in Chicago (September 3-6, 1955) and to release the funeral photographs to Jet magazine (September 15, 1955) is the deliberate act of public-witness that is widely credited with catalyzing the modern civil rights movement; Chicago Public Schools teacher (1960-1983); civil rights speaker for four decades after her son's murder.

photo.alt: Mamie Till viewing her son Emmett Till's body at the morgue, accompanied by her fiancé Gene Mobley, September 1955
photo.repository: Wikimedia Commons (originally published in Jet magazine, September 15, 1955)
photo.license: Public domain (U.S., published 1931-1963 without copyright renewal)

ai_reading: Till-Mobley is referenced eight times across the catalog, more than any other not-itself-interviewed figure tracked here. The references cluster around the Bassett joint interview (entry 38, where the Bassetts met at a 1955 protest raising funds for her), the Wheeler Parker Jr. (entry 125) and Simeon Wright (entry 138) eyewitness pages, and the Emmett Till external_figure page. She is not herself a Civil Rights History Project interviewee (her death in January 2003 predated the project by seven years). Her standalone catalog page is added because the catalog's Mississippi-and-NAACP cohort cannot be fully resolved without the woman whose September 1955 decision to release the funeral photographs is the deliberate-public-witness act that recruited many of the corpus's later organizers; her own catalog page is the institutional-credit corrective to the literature that often centers her son's death without the witnessing-strategy decision that gave the death its public force. The novel observation her page surfaces is the deliberate-publicity signature: the September 1955 funeral was a strategic communications decision, not an automatic public event; Mamie Till-Mobley insisted on the open casket against the white Mississippi funeral director's objection and against the Chicago Cook County funeral director's hesitation, then released the photographs to David Jackson at Jet on September 15, and the resulting national circulation was the deliberate consequence of her choices.
biographical_paragraph: Mamie Carthan was born November 23, 1921 in Webb, Mississippi to Wiley Nash and Alma Carthan; her family moved to Argo, Illinois (the same Argo community Wheeler Parker Jr. and Simeon Wright, the Till cousins documented in catalog entries 125 and 138, settled in) when she was two. She graduated valedictorian of Argo Community High School in 1940 and married Louis Till in 1940; their son Emmett was born July 25, 1941. Louis Till died in U.S. Army custody in Italy in 1945. After Emmett's murder in August 1955 she insisted on the open-casket funeral at Roberts Temple Church of God in Christ in Chicago, September 3-6, 1955 (an estimated 50,000 mourners passed by the casket), and released the funeral photographs to David Jackson at Jet magazine, the issue that ran September 15, 1955. She returned to teach in Chicago Public Schools across 1960-1983. She remarried Gene Mobley in 1957 (the man visible alongside her in the morgue photograph). Her 2003 memoir Death of Innocence: The Story of the Hate Crime That Changed America (with Christopher Benson, One World/Random House) is the canonical first-person account of her decisions in September 1955 and the four-decade civil rights speaking career that followed. She died January 6, 2003 in Chicago at eighty-one.

sources.title: Mamie Till-Mobley and Christopher Benson, Death of Innocence: The Story of the Hate Crime That Changed America
sources.publisher: One World / Random House, 2003 (reprinted Random House Trade Paperbacks, 2004); WorldCat OCLC 51985418
